preFix.title: Woven tote bag on a frame loom
preFix.subtitle: A 35 × 30 cm tote bag woven in thick cotton warp and weft on a frame loom, joined at the sides after finishing
preFix.excerpt: Two woven panels, each 35 × 30 cm, are worked on a frame loom in plain weave using medium-weight cotton rope, then joined at the sides and base with a ladder stitch. Cotton handles are braided and secured at the top of each panel.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from standard frame loom weaving techniques and public-domain bag construction references.

content.text: Before the draft, here is a plain-English description of how the frame loom works for this project. A frame loom is a rectangular frame with nails or notches at regular intervals along the top and bottom edges. The
warp
is wound from top nail to bottom nail in a continuous figure-of-eight, or straight up and down, to create the vertical threads. The weaver then passes the
weft
over and under the warp threads on each row, alternating the over-under order on each
pick
to produce
plain weave
. This project weaves two separate panels, which are then joined to form a bag.
The
sett

for 3 to 4 mm cotton rope on a frame loom is 4 ends/cm (approximately 10 EPI). Each panel is 35 cm wide and 30 cm tall, requiring 35 cm × 4 ends/cm = 140 warp ends per panel (70 lengths of continuous warp wound up and back). Two panels are woven separately on the same loom, one after the other. Each finished panel will be approximately 35 × 30 cm after removal from the loom.

items.name: Medium-weight cotton rope or twine, 3 to 4 mm diameter
Frame loom, at least 35 × 35 cm internal weaving area
Stick shuttle or wide craft stick
Blunt darning needle for weaving in ends
Tapestry comb or fork beater for packing weft rows

content.text: The draft

definition.title: Frame loom plain weave: tote bag panel

content.text: Reading the draft: shaft 1 represents odd-numbered warp threads and shaft 2 represents even-numbered warp threads. The weaver alternates over-under across the warp on each row: over the odds and under the evens on one pass, then over the evens and under the odds on the next. This is plain weave. On a frame loom, the shed is opened by sliding a flat stick sideways through the warp, then turned on its edge to create the
shed
opening for the shuttle to pass through.
Warping and weaving the panels
Step 1: Set up the warp

Set the nails or notches on the frame loom at 4 per cm (approximately 10 per inch) across the top and bottom edges. Wind the cotton rope up over the top nail, down to the bottom nail, and back up to the next nail, continuing across the 35 cm width. This creates 140 warp threads. Maintain a firm, even tension throughout. Tie the warp rope to the first and last nail with a secure half-hitch.
Step 2: Weave the panel

Slide a flat stick (the shed stick) through the warp, passing it over every odd warp thread and under every even warp thread. Turn the stick on its edge to open the first shed and pass the shuttle through from left to right. Leave a 5 cm tail at the
selvedge
on the first row.

For the second row, remove the stick, then weave the shuttle back from right to left passing over each warp thread that was under on the first row, and under each that was over. Use a weaving comb or fork to beat each row down firmly before the next row. Pack the rows so there is no visible warp: this is a weft-faced plain weave, which gives the cotton rope its characteristic woven-basket appearance.

Continue weaving until the panel reaches 30 cm in height. Weave a tail of 5 cm at the last row and cut, leaving a tail to weave in. Weave both weft tails back into the body using a blunt darning needle, running each tail along a row for at least 3 cm.
Step 3: Remove panel and weave the second

Lift the panel off the nails carefully, keeping the warp loops intact. The warp loops at the top and bottom of each panel become the finished edges of the bag: they do not need to be hemmed. Re-warp the loom and weave the second panel to match.
Finishing
Step 4: Join the panels
Place the two panels with the weft faces together and align the edges. Join the two side edges and the base edge with a
ladder stitch

using a length of the same cotton rope and a blunt darning needle. Work along the right side seam from top to base, then along the base, then up the left side seam. Pull the seam firm but not so tight that the panels bunch. Turn the bag right side out.
Step 5: Make and secure the handles

Cut 6 lengths of cotton rope, each 130 cm. Divide into two groups of 3. Braid each group firmly into a handle cord approximately 60 cm long. Knot each end of the braid to secure. Position one handle on each panel: space the two knot ends 10 cm apart at the top edge of the panel, centred across the width. Pass each knotted end through the top warp loops of the panel and knot securely on the inside. Alternatively, fold each knotted end to the inside of the panel and stitch in place with several passes of cotton rope through the woven body.

items.solution: The two panels must be woven to the same warp length and with the same pick count. Count rows as you weave and match the second panel exactly to the first.
The weft is being pulled too tight at the edges. Leave a longer arc and ease the weft toward the selvedge before beating each row. Cotton rope at 4 ends/cm needs a generous arc.
The beat is too light. Use the tapestry comb more firmly after each row. The weft should completely cover the warp in a weft-faced structure.

items.problem: Bag distorts when turned right side out
items.solution: The ladder stitch seam is too tight on one side. Remove the seam thread and re-stitch with slightly less tension.
